title: Voucher redemption rate
template: SELECT v.voucher_code, v.issued_count, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) as redeemed, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) * 1.0 / NULLIF(v.issued_count, 0) as redemption_rate FROM vouchers v LEFT JOIN orders o ON v.voucher_code = o.voucher_code GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY redemption_rate DESC
anti_patterns: computing redemption_rate as redemptions / total_orders instead of redemptions / vouchers_issued — denominator must be how many were distributed
not LEFT JOINing — INNER JOIN drops never-redeemed codes and inflates the rate

title: Average discount depth by campaign
template: SELECT voucher_campaign, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) as orders, SUM(discount_amount) as total_discount, SUM(order_total_before_discount) as gross_revenue, AVG(discount_amount / NULLIF(order_total_before_discount, 0)) as avg_discount_depth FROM orders WHERE voucher_code IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY avg_discount_depth DESC
anti_patterns: averaging percentages instead of computing aggregate_discount / aggregate_revenue — per-order averages are distorted by small orders with large flat discounts

title: Net revenue impact of discount codes
template: WITH promo AS (SELECT voucher_code, SUM(order_total_before_discount) as gross_revenue, SUM(discount_amount) as total_discount, SUM(order_total_after_discount) as net_revenue, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) as orders FROM orders WHERE voucher_code IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1), baseline AS (SELECT AVG(order_total_after_discount) as avg_order_no_promo FROM orders WHERE voucher_code IS NULL) SELECT p.*, b.avg_order_no_promo, net_revenue - (orders * b.avg_order_no_promo) as incremental_revenue_vs_baseline FROM promo p CROSS JOIN baseline b
anti_patterns: treating all discounted revenue as additive — some orders would have happened without a code (cannibalisation)

title: Voucher incrementality — new spend vs cannibalised spend
template: WITH voucher_users AS (SELECT DISTINCT customer_id FROM orders WHERE voucher_code IS NOT NULL AND order_date BETWEEN :start AND :end), pre_voucher AS (SELECT o.customer_id, AVG(o.order_total_after_discount) as avg_order_pre FROM orders o JOIN voucher_users v USING(customer_id) WHERE o.order_date < :start AND o.voucher_code IS NULL GROUP BY 1), post_voucher AS (SELECT o.customer_id, AVG(o.order_total_after_discount) as avg_order_post FROM orders o JOIN voucher_users v USING(customer_id) WHERE o.order_date BETWEEN :start AND :end GROUP BY 1) SELECT AVG(avg_order_post - avg_order_pre) as avg_incremental_spend_per_customer FROM pre_voucher JOIN post_voucher USING(customer_id)
anti_patterns: comparing voucher vs non-voucher users without controlling for selection bias — customers who seek vouchers are not representative of the overall base

title: Voucher abuse detection (multiple redemptions)
template: SELECT customer_id, email, COUNT(DISTINCT voucher_code) as codes_used, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) as promo_orders, COUNT(DISTINCT device_fingerprint) as devices, COUNT(DISTINCT shipping_address_hash) as addresses, CASE WHEN COUNT(DISTINCT voucher_code) > 3 OR COUNT(DISTINCT device_fingerprint) = 1 AND COUNT(DISTINCT email) > 2 THEN 'high_risk' WHEN COUNT(DISTINCT voucher_code) > 1 THEN 'review' ELSE 'ok' END as abuse_flag FROM orders WHERE voucher_code IS NOT NULL AND order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - 90 GROUP BY 1, 2 HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT voucher_code) > 1
anti_patterns: flagging only by email — abusers use disposable emails; device fingerprint and shipping address are stronger signals

title: First-time vs repeat use of vouchers
template: WITH voucher_orders AS (SELECT customer_id, order_id, order_date, voucher_code, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) as customer_order_rank, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id, voucher_code ORDER BY order_date) as voucher_use_rank FROM orders WHERE voucher_code IS NOT NULL) SELECT voucher_code, COUNT(CASE WHEN customer_order_rank = 1 THEN 1 END) as new_customer_uses, COUNT(CASE WHEN customer_order_rank > 1 THEN 1 END) as existing_customer_uses, COUNT(CASE WHEN voucher_use_rank > 1 THEN 1 END) as repeat_same_code_uses FROM voucher_orders GROUP BY 1
when_to_use: new vs existing customer voucher use
anti_patterns: using account creation date instead of order history to classify new vs existing — a registered user with no prior purchases is a new buyer

title: Discount stacking detection
template: SELECT order_id, customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT discount_type) as discount_types_applied, SUM(discount_amount) as total_discount, order_total_before_discount, SUM(discount_amount) * 1.0 / NULLIF(order_total_before_discount, 0) as effective_discount_rate FROM order_discounts JOIN orders USING(order_id) GROUP BY order_id, customer_id, order_total_before_discount HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT discount_type) > 1 ORDER BY effective_discount_rate DESC
anti_patterns: grouping by discount_type without grouping by order_id — misses the per-order stacking view

title: Time to redemption after voucher issue
template: SELECT v.campaign_id, COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id) as redemptions, AVG(DATE_DIFF('day', v.issued_at, o.order_date)) as avg_days_to_redeem, PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY DATE_DIFF('day', v.issued_at, o.order_date)) as median_days_to_redeem, COUNT(CASE WHEN DATE_DIFF('day', v.issued_at, o.order_date) <= 3 THEN 1 END) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) as redeemed_within_3d FROM voucher_issuances v JOIN orders o ON v.voucher_code = o.voucher_code GROUP BY 1
anti_patterns: using mean days-to-redemption for skewed distributions — use median; a handful of late redemptions can inflate mean significantly

title: A/B comparison of voucher codes / discount amounts
template: SELECT voucher_code, discount_pct, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) as users, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) as orders, SUM(order_total_after_discount) as net_revenue, SUM(discount_amount) as discount_cost, SUM(order_total_after_discount) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0) as revenue_per_user, SUM(discount_amount) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id), 0) as discount_cost_per_user FROM orders WHERE voucher_code IN (:code_a, :code_b) GROUP BY 1, 2
anti_patterns: comparing codes without controlling for customer segment — a 15% code sent to VIPs vs 10% to new users is not a fair comparison
